title: Crossville chronicle. [volume]

text:
The Ocmocx-at.
A. 3UGUEGOK, K.litor.
The Pokpr-Pontl legislature proposes
to adjourn next Montlay.
The Repository is earnestly laboring
for MeKinley and fourth term.
The Cincinnati Commercial wants
free beer oil Sunday, and is denouncing
Sunday laws.
Four bills have been introduced into
the Legislature to amend the Pond
liquor tax law.
It is said that General Grant is a
'Presidential quantity. It may turn out
that Samuel J.Tllden is also.
Mayor Lawthers, of Youngstown,
was re-elected and had only a majority
of 1,019 in that Republican stronghold.
It is evident by the Chinese veto
message of President Arthur that he is
a firm believer in the doctrines of the
celebrated Morey letter.
Senator Pendleton thinks the re
sult in Cincinnati but the beginning of
the end. The protection doctrine of the
Republican party did the work.
The Star Route indictments are held
by Judge Wylie at Washington, to be
good. Porsey not appearing his bail
was forfeited and warrant issued for
his arrest.
According to wise Republican papers,
like the Repository, the Democrats must
have "blundered aga'n" on the 3d inst.,
especially in Cincinnati, Cleveland,
Toledo and elsewhere.
Last fall in Cincinnati the boss brew
ers resolved in convention that it was
to their interest to vote the Republican
ticket, and they did. They reconsid
ered and did better on the 3d inst.
The late elections in Ohio will give
the Democracy a show for next fall.
The aim should be to place in nomina
tion for Congress. State and county po
siMons, ;nen of character who have the
confidence of the people. Let us have
men of principle as our principal men.
In California there are forty-three
male adult coolie laborers and mechanics
to everv one hundred white male adults.
This condition of things in Ohio would
wake iip our laboring men, and settle
the fnte of every Chinese member of
Congress, and send the Chinese Republi
can party to kingdom come.
The Republicans in Washington were
quite rhon-fallen last week over the re
sult of the local elections. At Colum
bus ditto, Cincinnati. Cleveland, Toledo,
&.Q. I'pspfting Republican calculations
the way they did on the 3d inst. is any
thing but encouraging to the leaders of
the Chinese party. t
i
According to the Cleveland Herald's
special from Washington under date of
the Cth, Major MeKinley made a g-r-e-a-t
tariff speech. We knew it would come
about this time io help the Major's
nomination. The Major don't believe
in equal taxation. Protection monopo
lists don't believe in justice and equalty.
The Democrats had a sweeping victory
in Chicago last week, carryin g the Coun
cil over both the Republicans and the
Socialists. This is the first time for
years the Democrats have secured con
trol of that branch of the city govern
ment. On the general ticket the aver
age Democratic majority in the city is
about 4,n.
Since the election in Cincinnati the
Commercial of that town wants the Pond
law amended by cutting down the
license. That paper says it is a tax, not
a license. But the law orders the shut
ting up of all saloons that don't pay,
hence it is a license tax, that is a privi
lege tax. I If they don't pay they shall
not do business.
The Washington Sentinel remarks:
"Our German Republicans have once
more discovered that that much abused
Democratic party is after all the party of
liberty and the Republican party that of
despotism and slavery." They ought to
have learned it in the days'.of Knownoth
ingism. Black Republican Freheit came
then with a brickbat.
Excessive high tariff is yielding the
usual results high wages, high prices
of rents and provisions, strikes and dis
satisfaction. There were seventeen
hundred tailors in one meeting the
mother night in New York who hae $18,
000 in the Treasury. The Elevated
Railroad people are about to strike, and
the Locomotive Builders Association.
The Chinese bill passed the Senate by
the votes of twenty Democrats and nine
Republicans. In the House it had the
votes of one hundred and nine Democruts
and fifty-eight Republicans, with a ne
gative vote of sixty-one Republicans and
four Democrats. Arthur vetoes the meas
ure and it failed to receive a three-
fourths vote, and hence, is killed. Work
incrmen should stick a pin in the Re
publican coffin for this.
A Canton dispatch appeared in the
dallies last week, stating that Canton
township went Republican. That dis.
patcher was too ignorant to know that
the city is included in the township, and
votes for township officers, and that
hence the Democratic ticket was elected
except one Trustee. These fellows should
disnatch the trnth. Oar friends in tne
township outside of the city are some
times negligent and allow the Republi
. cans to. get away with the township
poll, wherJt is wholly unnecessary.-
THE CHINESE QUESTION.
As our readers know, Presideut Arthur
last week vetoed the bill restricting
Chinese Coolie immigration for twenty
years. The bill had passed both branches
of Congress by large majorities and Is
approved by nine-tenths of the American
people. Notwithstanding this, Arthur,
as did the fraudulent Hayes, vetoes the
measure.
To meet Hayes's objections, given in
his veto, Congress authorized a special
embassy to China to negotiate a treaty
conceding our right to regulate and
suspend Coolie immigration. The treaty
was ratified by both Governments, and
the law Arthur has just vetoed was
passed in pursuance of it. It had the
support of the best legal minds in both
the Senate and House, and passed the
former by very nearly and the latter
bodv bv over two-thirds majority. The
veto was sustained in the Semte,
the bill not receiving a two-
thirds vote, being 29 for, to 21 against
President Arthur's techincal and legal
objections were urged in Congress by
Chinese members while the bill was
undir discuislon, and were completely
controverted. He champions this Coolie
immigration of servile labor and says:
"But if there is too much of it in one
"section of the country the same labor
"mfty be needed in another section." As
the Pacific States and territories are
over-run by this cheap form of servile
labor, this is equivalent to a declaration
that it should be transplanted to other
States and spread over the country.
Let mechanics and laboring men con
sWer this. This is the very doctrine
proclaimed in the Morey letter about
which Republicans made such a fuss
and over which they were so indignant.
Do these men want to enslave labor? If
they do not why do they want the Coolie
system Introduced?
Laboring men will hardly agree with
Arthur that, "Chinese labor has been of
"great value to this country." At least,
workingmen in California will not
agree with this proposition. At an elec
tion held last year on the question of
prohibiting Chinese immigration, the
vote of that State stood 154,000 in favor
of such prohibition, and only 1,100
votes could be obtained In favor of the
immigration on a secret ballot. This
shows the recorded judgment of worthy
citizens after a long and bitter experi
eute. In California are over 73,000 Chinese,
and of these 70,000 are adult males, or 43
per cent, to the white male adults of
the State; In Idaho the per centage is 22,
in Nevada and Oregon 10 each and in
Washington 11. This is an average of
23 per cent., or rather more than one in
five. President Arthur seems to think
it would be a good thing to have this
proportion of Coolie serfs in competition
with the white labor of Ohio. What do
workingmen think about it?
The message refers to the importance
of our commercial relations. with China;
but this is all on one side, as statistics
show the exports of the United States to
China in 1881 amounted to $S,3t 1,537,
while the imports from China to the
United States were $21,717,550. In other
words, we pay them three dollars for
every dollar they pay us. Where are
the benefits?
It is claimed that the influence of the
great Pacific railway corporations had
something to do in bringing about this
veto. They lobbied against the bill for
they want cheap labor that don't strike.
The Six Companies of San Francisco and
China, that operate in the buying and
selling of Coolie flesh and blood, had a
hired lobby in Washington working to
defeat the bill. They make money out
of the traffic and wish it to continue as
did the Yankee ship owners who used to
import negro slaves from Africa.
There Is another class whose mawkish
philanthropy makes them advocates of
Chiueso immigration. These express
their horror at Mormon polygamy, but
are both willing and anxious to flood
the country with an abominable Chinese
population whose immoralities in all
these respects are well known. Rev.
Henry Ward Beecher is one of this class,
and he has quite a following, of course.
The worst forms of diseased conditions
come with these Cool e , but it seems to
constitute no objection with these
philanthropists."
Irrespective of politics or religion, the
press of California pronounce the veto
violent assumption of power, and this
is the sentiment of nearly all the citi
zens beyond the Rocky Mountains with
out regard to political affiliations. They
denounce the party that ignores the
rights of free American laboring men
in order to obey the commrnds of a few
gready railway lords 'and contractors of
New England. It is time this degrading
labor by monopoly arrangements, au
thorized and sustained by Government,
were properly understood and properly
met. We do not believe in re-opening
the slave trade in any shape. The
American people are opposed to it, nine
out of ten.no matter whether the hordes
are negroes or Coolies.
John Sherman thinks the excitement
in California over the veto of the Chi
nese bill should be treated with con
tempt, and that the Senate should take
its time in going about further legisla
tion in that direction. The active part
Mr. Sherman took in the forgery and
perjury game, by which Hayes could be
fraudulently counted in as President,
shows that he has very little respect for
the will of the people.
The Republican party of Ohio will
contest next fall's campaign by advo
cating Chinese coolie cheap labor to
drive out American workingmen, and in
defending the Poker-Pond Legislature.
The Democracy will be fornenst them
there.
Rhode Island on the 5th, held their
State election and the manufacturing
mononolv lords had things their
own way, electing A. W. Llttlefleld
Governor, and a Legislature of the right
color to return Senator Anthony.
THE MOREY LETTER.
Near the close of the Garfield campaign
in 1S80, assisted Garfield's election. He
called it a forgery and said "its stupid
and brutal sentiments I never expressed
or entertained." It was a "good enough
Morgan until after election." Let us
see what were these "stupid and brutal
Bentiments" according to General Gar
field. Here are the passages:
I take it that the question of em
ployes is only a question of private
and corporate economy, and individuals
or companies have the right to buy la
bor where they can get it cheapest. We
have a treaty with the Chinese Govern
ment which should be religiously kept
uutil its provisions are abrogated by the
action of the general Government, and
I am not prepared to say that it should
be abrogated uutll our great manufac
turing interests are conserved in the
matter of labor.
President Arthur in his veto of the
anti-Chinese bill takes substantially
the same position. His veto Is sustained
by the party so that laboring men are
not entitled to "protection;" but mo
nopoly capitalists are. This is the Re
publican doctrine.
I fOSl
The new Constitution of California
was submitted to the people for adop
tion, in April, 1879, only three years
ago, and a proposition to prohibit Chi
nese immigration was separately sub
mitted, with this result:
For prohibition of the Chinese
AgaltMt prohibition ....
Nuu-cuuuuiltul
.1S4,S9
. B.Bftl
Prohibition majority over all 147,814
Of more . than 100,000 votes cast at
that election only the insignificant
handful of 8S3 were cast in favor of
Chinese immigration ino that State.
Such are the views and feelings of the
whole mass of the people of California,
Irrespective of party, after thirty years'
trial of the benefits of Chinese civiliza
tion, habits, end cheap labor. Against
the knowledge and experience of the
whole people of California, and we may
include Oregon, Washington and Ne
vada, Mr. Arthur sets up his individual
ignorance and inexperience!
Terrible crimes have been commit
ted in Ireland lately, which chill the
blood of human people everywhere.
Evil, however, begets evil everywhere.
The N. Y. Herald's London special, re
ferring to the murder of the lady re
cently killod in an attempt on the life
of her brother-in-law, a large land pro
prietor, says:
Fearful as was this assassination and
that of Mr. Herbert, the English public
continues to view comparatively with
complacency the dreadful scenes en
acted daily on the lonely and bleak
mountain sides, where cavalry, in
fantry and constabulary evict from
land held at fifty per cent, above the
valuation the miserable creatures
whose bodies and souls can scarcely be
held together. Who can wonder that
the exasperation of such sights has pro
duced that terrible figure in modern
history, the masked assassin?
Last week President Arthur nomi
nated ex-Senator Teller, of California,
to be Secretary of the Interior in place
ofKirkwood, and Win. E. Chandler, of
New Hampshire, for Secretary of the
Navy In place of Hunt, who Is appointed
Minister to Russia. The President also
nominated John Jay Knox for Comp
troller of the Currency, and named Ro
land Worthington for Collector, Aden B.
Underwood for Surveyor, and Daniel
Hall for Naval Officer at Boston. Mr.
Chandler was the first Republican of
note to speak out his mind about the
fraud and sham of R. B. nayes. True,
he did not speak till after the inaugura
tion, and in faot, like his namesake,
Zach, he promoted the great crime, as
did Garfield, Sherman and the rest.
The Democrats of the Senate illus
trated the tenacity with which their
party clings to its disgraceful race
prejudices by voting in solid phalanx to
sustain the Chinese restriction bill
with all its obnoxious features. Had
the bill been still more obnoxious and
oppressive, they. would have sustained
it all tne more coruiauy. Having no
longer a "nigger" to hato, the party is
ereedv for a new victim. Ohio State
Journal.
And now "the party of great moral
ideas" and infinite hypocrisy and fraud,
desire to take to their embrace Chinese
Coolie slavery with its barbarism, po
lygamy and disease. They are not sat
isfied with loving the "nigger." They
desire a worse monster to worship and
fondle.
Arthur has just had a grand admin
istrative triumph, in prosecuting his
policy that every person in the public
service who cannot render efficient aid
to the Republican machine must be
driven from place. The story is told in
this Washington dispatch:
Mrs. Wilcox, a daughter of Andrew
Jackson Donaldson and widow of Gen
eral Wilcox of Tennessee, has been re
moved from the money order otlice of
the Postofflce Department, at the In
stance of Representatives liouk and
Moore. She was appointed to the posi
tion by Judge Key when Postmaster
Gnneral. Mrs. Wilcox is a grand niece
of Andrew Jackson and was tne only
child ever born in tne wnne iiouse.
Hon. K. N. Hartshorn called at the
Review Den last Monday. Like every
other good temperance man he stands
bv the "fona Jjiquor i,aw. vuiy toe
saloon keepers and the cranlry Prohibi
tlonlsts object to it. Alliance Review.
Indeed I And the Stark county mem
bers in the House, Messrs, Conrad and
Snyder, voted also for the "Pond Liquor
Law;" "like good temperance men.'
They are all "good temperance men,'
and not "cranky, even it tney ao taxe
it straight.
There is considerable talk of the
nmoirata of New York running Tilden
for Governor next fall. It is generally
conceded that New York will go Demo
cratic this year, and if Tilden is nomi
rt atari an ,1 i noted Governor it will place
him on the high road to the Presidency
in 1884. Lt. uiairsvuie uazene.

BACK $4 WAGE REDUCTION
NEW HAVEN MASTER BAKERS
GRANT INCREASE DEMANDED BY MEN
Bakers Recover Reduction Forced On Them By
Arbitration a Year Ago With" Few Excep
tions Shops Are Working Under
Normal Conditions.
Restoration of the approximately $4
weekly which had been taken from
the bakers last year and the waiving
Of minor oemanas pertaining to tne
settlement of the new agreement en-
tered into, between the Bakery and
Confectionery Workers International
Union- and the bakery owners 01
' New Haven at a conference of rep
resentatives of both factions held last
Tuesday.," Th .'new agreement was
ratified at a special meeting of the
bakery worker? held, in Trades Coun
cil hall last Wednesday.
Unlike last year when the question
was brought before a board of arbi
tration before a settlement could be
reached the conferences held this year
were marked by the friendly feelings
displayed by both sides and most of
the bakery owners had signed the
new agreement before the old one
had expired on May 1.
A few, however, had remained out
side the fold and In such cases the
men worked under the old agreement
while the subject of wages and work
ing conditions were under discus-
men were insistent was that of hav
ing the wages which were taken from
them last year restored as there has
been considerable dissension in the
ranks of the bakers ever since that
famous decision was rendered.
Under a clause in the contract all
differences arising between the bakers
and bakery owners must be settled
by an equal number of men repre
senting both factions and one disin-
-cfn4 nprenn T act vpir n ft pr -fail-
ure to arrive at a settlement the mat
ter was brought before the board of
arbitration with the Rev. James
McGee of Calvary Baptist church act
ing as umpire.
Reviewing -the contentions of both
sides Rev. McGee stated his belief
that a fair and just settlement could
only be made by the willingness ot
both parties to share the existing con
ditions. As the Master Bakers had
asked for a cut of $5 in the wages
of the men he divided it so that both
sides could share some of the bur
den spoken of and slapped a 10 per

ed politicians. Their union has

New York, May 4. Wage increases

the International Seamen's Union.
This may have no significance to the
average reader of the daily press, but

started a lockout against this union two
years ago. The eight-hour day was
abolished, wages were reduced and a
general anti-union policy assumed.

vessel owners talked about the need for

ponents of two years ago have learned
no lesson.

to beg for terms just as she has had
to at the conclusion of others of her wars. That Russia has enormous
resources is of course undisputed but having enormous rccourccs and
possessing the facilities to use them arc two very different matters in
deed and it is hi the latter detail that Russia seems doomed to failure.
Naturally enough when peace is discussed speculation is indulged in
as to what Japan's demands may be and it seems safe to say that the
victors will exercise as much diplomacy in making peace as they have
exercised courage and patience in making war. They will profit by
the lessons of the past and while a large cash indemnity will surely be
demanded it is by no means certain that it will be insisted upon. On
the other hand it is certain that Japan will regain the island of Sag
lialien which was actually stolen from her by Russia. She will retain
control of Korea and Will rcassumc possession of the Liao Tung pen
insula, which was actually ceded to her by China as fruit of the victory
in 1894. Manchuria will probably be restored to China under a meas
ure of Japanese protection and there, in all probability the transfer ol
territory will cease.
That it is not wise to be too harsh with a beaten enemy is a maxim of
modem diplomacy as events in the past century have shown.
In the remarkable memoirs of Dc Blowitz, that dean of correspond
ents writes of a conversation with the man of "blood and iron," Princo
Bismarck, in relation to the harsh terms imposed by the treaty of San
Stefano. Bismarck said:
"The peace of San Stefano was one of the most thoughtless actions
of modern history. Ignatieff made a blunder which no true statesman
would ever have committed. He took everything that he could get.
When an enemy is vanquished and one has one's foot on his neck, he
can be made to give whatever one wants, but one must think of the
consequences of the victory as wcjl as the consequences of the defeat..
We should not be where we are now if, in 1866, I had taken territory
from Austria- I had said when we started, 'If we should be victorious
tl shall not annex any Austrian territory, for we must not remain en
emies for ever.' When we were victorious everyone wanted me to take
territory from her. I held my own and I have often had cause to con
gratulate myself that I did so."
Naturally enough de Blowitz thought of the taking of Alsace and
Lorraine by Germany in 187 1, but Bismarck rejoined:
"In 1871 I acted in the same way. France Avas in our hands. Paris
was conquered, the Commune was brewing, everything was disorgan
ized; if I had acted like Ignatieff I should have demanded Picardy and
Champagne. When 1 was urged to take Belfort and Metz I refused,
saying 'No. Belfort is in the hands of the French, it must remain
theirs.' Even with regard to Metz, on seeing the despair of poor M.
Thiers but I was obliged' to listen to Moltkc who kept repeating that
iMetz meant a difference of 100,000 men more or less in the army. 1
could not impose upon my country the burden of putting 100,000 more
men into active service at a moment."
Of course every German looked on Alsace and Lorraine as German
soil and their transfer to Germany was regarded not as despoiling
France but as a rightful restoration. France has not even to the pres
ent day been able to look at it in that light, but that is aside from the
issue. Bismarck's cry for leniency was the result of the closest study
of men and nations and there would seem to be little doubt that Japan
will take heed to the warning. It is not to be conceived, for instance,
that Japan will think of acquiring Vladivostok as the result of the war
It may be reduced as a fortress but it will surely remain Russian.

it is to presumed, very similar
conditions. One cause of trouble on the plantations is said 'to be mis
understandings between the laborers and the overseers, due to the fact
that they seldom understand each other perfectly. Rev. J. W. Barton
who is in charge of the Methodist missions to the Hindus in Fiji, gives
the following interesting account of conditions there, and of the cause
of many misunderstandings, and makes a suggestion that might bo
found useful here. That suggestion is that foremen and overseers
should be able to talk understandingly with the laborers under them
Says the Rev. Barton:
"The coolie comes to Fiji under very strict government supervision.
From the time he leaves Calcutta until his return the Fiji government
is in loco-parentis. The government imports him and charges the cost
about $100 to the planter. The planter has then the coolie under
indenture for a period of five years, during which time he receives the
stipulated wage of is. per day or per task. At the end of five years
the coolie is free to engage himself in whatsoever way he choose.
When the five years of this free life are ended he may return, passage
paid, to his own country, or else settle in Fiji permanently. The latter
alternative the great majority chooses, less than 25 per cent returning
Of course there are sometimes abuses in connection with this coolie
labor, and many English people would shudder at the condition under
which they live and work. The department responsible for the coolie
the Immigration Department does its best, I believe, to eliminate
these abuses, but a coolie inspector cannot be everywhere, and the
coolie is such a notorious liar that his complaints cannot be much
heeded by the average Englishman. One of the reforms which seems
urgently needed is some law which would compel the overseer or
planter who works coolie labor to pass an examination in the Hindu
stani language. Some of the overseers speak exceedingly good collo
quial Hindustani, and such have little difficulty with their labor. Oth
ers make but little attempt to learn the language, and trust to the sir
dar, who speaks pigeon English. The result is misunderstanding, and
sometimes bloodshed. Such men bring vicarious suffering upon over
seers that are honestly trying to do the right thing. The position of
overseer is a most unenviable one. There is on the one hand the coolie
difficult to manage, sometimes lazy, often obstreperous, and on the
other there is the Fijian Company trying to reduce the cost of cane
production to a minimum. The compulsory working of the women in
the canefields is another abuse that a few years' advance in public opin
ion will make impossible'

According to the stories told of the trials of
th.- coolies taken from China to Cuba and Peru,
the situation of the coolie in a British colony
was a bod of roses by comparison. Shipload
after shipload of Chinese coolies was Bent to
Cuba and Peru, bound to serve t!ie planters for

were kidnapped or Induced to engage under
false pretences. Tales of poorly equipped and
badly overcrowded transport ships were re

This drove the Peruvian trade In cooHaa to the

Colon. Dee. 2». IWl-If the American
plan* for an interocetnlc canal offer no
serious or forbidding engineering diffi
culties. there are other problems grave
In character that we shall have to solve.
Foremost among these Is that of apply
ing the laws of sanitation to the Isthmus
and of forcing obedience to these laws.
Of that problem 1 have already written
exhaustively. The other most serious
one deals with the question of labor.
There hah been, over since the republic
was established, a very determined agita
tion against the importation of Chinese
coolies for the canal work. The argu
ment Is advanced that the lathmus and
any other country outside of China. The
item of clothing te almost entirely elimi
nated from theft- expense accounts. Here,
as In China, they conduct their little
"pigeons” In the front of the buildings
they Occupy as residence*. Fuel, except
for small charcoal Area for cooking, la
never required, and an able-bodied Chi
naman can feed hlmeelf on the isthmus
at the expense Of a few pennies a day.
In consequence they can undersell any of
the other merchants on the isthmus, es
pecially as they import largely and di
rectly from China. These advantages,
with that clannishness characteristic of
the yellow race, strike terror to the hearts
curia* this cUm of laborers. The for
mer effort* to construct till* canal result
ed in * honey depopulation *f the West
Indian Islands. Should we determine te
use these nsgroes In our attempt we could
easily exhaust those islands of able-bod
ied men. Their chempions say they can
be secured In limitless quantities. That
Is entirely false. When we have trap
pled with the task of building the canal
and gotten it under full swing we shall
need almost Innumerable thousands of
laborers. The moat optimistic estimates
are that with 20,000 men we can complete
the waterway in eight years. It is more
reasonable to preaume that 80,000 men will
have it ready to open In ten yean, for
If there Is one thing we must avoid, if
we would succeed where the French fall
ed. It Is the undue optimism which cursed
the whole French effort. Should we de
termine to proeeeute the work steadily
through every twenty-four hours of the
day by using electric lights at night—
e thing which, by the way. engineers and
sanitary experts recommend—tho time re
quired for building the canal might be
reduced one-half, but the corps of labor
ers required would be trebled in slae.
It we estimate the number In this
corps by taking the mean of the estl
mates given, respectively, by the optim
istic and the pessimistic, we shall prob
ably hit about the figure fact will demon
strate set necessary. This will be 36,000
man. with constant recruits to till the va
cancies left by disease and death, with

the labor necessary. This is. Of course,
fatuous. It does not, however, deter the
leading spirits in the republic front very

The Star and Herald, official organs of
the republic, has demanded editorially the

This opposition is purely selfish. There
Is no labor on the isthmus that would be

ence of Chinese; there is no society here

by the coolies. They are, however, a se

they now control fn a large measure both

of the Caucasian merchant*. They know
that mllHons of dollars will be disbursed

they hope to accumulate. This fear is,

West Indians Poor Laborers.
The Panamaians say the United States
should employ native and West Indian
negro labor. That sounds like a sensible
proposition, but unfortunately the nega
tive contentlen weighs down the affirm
ative. In the first place, the native labor,
if such a thing can J>e said to exist, is
absolutely worthless. The native laborer
is iasy. shiftless. Irresponsible, and gen
erally of no account. Why should ho
toll in sun-baked or rain-flooded trencher
for eight hours a day when he can Uve
as well as he wishes to live by Idling all
the time, trapping his meat and going
the force* working an eight-hour shift
a day. One hundred thousand will be the
number If the working day Is divided Into
three eight-hour shifts. The West Indian
Islands could not supply the smaller num
ber to «ay nothing of the larger. Only
those West Indians to whom the wages
offered would prove a magnet could be
employed for this work; those who now
earn more on their native Island*
than the canal comtractora will
pay could not be Imported,
Now the standard rate of wage* for ordi
nary manual labor on the Isthmus Is
$3.20 (Columbian silver) a day. It will
probably never be any higher, for should
th* republic llx an arbitrary rate of ex
change at two for one. as is now pro
posed. this wage will be eqQal to $1.10 gold
a day. Certainly the contractor* on the
canal will not pay more than this sum.
Person* who have studied th* problem
of labor aupply In the West Indies from,
the standpoint of the wage likely to be
paid say that, even If the government* of
these Islands Interpose no objection, It
will not be possible to obtain more than

Vinol cures backing coughs, chronic

Vinol is sold everywhere on a positive

Into the forest and jungle for vegetable

As for the other plan—that of bringing
West Indian negroes to the Isthmus—It
is advanced without any intelligent idea
as to It* practicability. The stock argu

ing the period of active work by the
French every class of labor known to
large employers was called Into service,
and the West Indian negro proved more
efficient and better adapted to the cli
mate than did any other—tnat Is. for a
season. He did then, and will now. live
longer on the isthmus than will the Chi
nese. Mayalans. Africans. Syrians or any
other. For a Httle while he will work
steadier and accomplish more than will
the other classes, but his Inherent shift
lessness soon overcomes him in this en
ervating climate, and he lapses Into an
Idleness from which it Is almost Impossi
ble to arouse him. Add to this the fact
that he is naturally most immoral, court
ing rather than shunning the most loath
some afflictions, prone to debilitating ex
cess** and especially fond of dedicating
his nights to wild revelry and the worst
carouses, and It will be seen that the
temporary advantage resulting from his
labor is more than offset by the harm he
does himself and to society In general.
The Isthmus ha* as many of these Idle
and Improvident West Indiana as it has
any use for. To bring more would only
increase the sociological problem .that
will sooner or later have to be solved
Negroes Not Procurable.
Besides this phase of the West Indian

Tito Coolie a Steady Worker.
Another thing which Inclines experts to

reverse was found to be the case with

work In their lasy. ahiftleaa way, requir

they would Indulge, In the wildest orgies.
Night after night they would devote to

then there were their regular seasons of

The French practice was to pay on alter

With the canal In operation the railroad

Such are the general aspects of the

This might as well be done now as at any

text:
For the river people are a tabooed class.
Neither they nor their children for four
generatlons < after leaving the river may
hold office, and in every way they are
boycotted and ostracised to make their
existence a misery- All this just because
there is a tradition that once upon a time
some of the river people conspired against
the inland waters and sheltered inlets of
the coast. Whenever one of the3e
wretches loses the floating home which it
has taken a lifetime of savings to get.
his poverty is too terrible to contemplate,
and he usually plunges Into the «ea at
once to end his misery.
ne*g of the coolie breed3 hautrhty diadalfl
ami overbearing contempt in the masters,
who lord it over them with a dlpnity un
known outside of the land of the red pa
per gods.
Economy thoueh Is th<* grand virtue of
China, ami by their skillful parinsr of the
earth they have succeeded in rnbblnsr it
of every particle of richness arA mold,
until now over most of the tillable parts
of the empire the fertile soil ha* been
used up clean and stripped away, leaving
but the br.rren. naked clay, incapable
of producing" good crops without
unusual and h^avy fertilization, peculiar
methods of cultivation and excessive ir
rigation. Their economics of production
are only equaled by their economics of
consumption. The very grass roots are
pulled from the ground to serve as fuel
and for use in paper-making. Every bit
of vegetable fiber Is pressed Into servtco
and the soil Is robbed of its mulch. Scraps
of paper and cloth are paste-i together to
make shoe soles, bits of wood are glued
America and the vistas of wealtiTwhlch
he commands.
By feigning Insanity he has the delight
ful State hospitals for the Insane at his
service. A trifling crime will transport
him to a lovely seaside resort of the State,
with a flower gardened court, and Jus;:
enough labor to keep his appetite up to
an appreciation of the wholesome faro
provided. Should he scorn Insanity or
crime there Is still the comfortable poor
houBe open to him free of charge or care.
China makes no x such provision for
paupers. They may die and rot if they
cannot make their own living; there are
too many people anyway and the weakest
had best die off as soon as they can.
There Is not room on earth for them and
nobody is going to waste time coaxing
them to stay and lncumber the way for
hardier and stronger workers who with
health and muscle still have all they can
do to keep from getting pushed over the
edge.
The dragon well represents the imperial
Government of which It Is the symbol, a
hungry, ferocious, devouring monster,
clawing for all In sight and destroying
with breath of fire that which It cannot
secure to Itself.
No wonder that the paper gods of their
temples all have- devilishly malignant
faces. Fates are so unkind to the coolie .
that he can Imagine nothing but evil ta
exist among the unseen powers which
rule our being. Those who are crowded
off terra firma by the rampant land
lordism float\by millions In junks, house
boats and floating villages, which cover
the Government or some of Its official*.
Chinese women are. apparently suffer
ing from a similar tradition, for It Is said
by 6ome of the Chinese that women once
took up arms and attacked tha ruling
authorities, and for that reason It was
ordered that henceforth their feet should
bo crippled bo that there- might be- no
danger from them!" This, while an absurd
reason, seems more in line with Chinese
cowardice than any of the others which
are given for foot binding.
There Is consolation for the river folk
though In the fact that one acre of water
produces an infinite number of times
more food than an acre of land, for It has
three dimensions, while the land has but
two, and more than that the rivers have
the sea to draw from and there are con
tinually ascendfng them streams of food
fish from the Incalculable stores of the
ocean's depths. So the river people skim
the rivers of everything that Is In them,
using fish hunting birds, such as cor
morants, to search out such fish as may
have escaped the nets which are every
where cast without regard to time or sea
son. ,
Patience Is another virtue which the
coolies have run clear Into the ground by
its excessive exercise. That very nega
tive virtue has been the cause of those
who exercise It being more and more
overloaded, until the Impositions placed
upon them crush out their Individual lives
and will presently end their national exist
ence. ¦ Their patience has been provoca
tive of ferocity In their tormentors as the
mild sheep by running away Invites the
attack of dogs which had not otherwise
paid any attention, but by witnessing 1
such show of terror have thereby created
within them an appetite for mutton.
By the law of compensation the meek-
In the markets of Tungchow, to which
the stewards of the noble families of Pe
king repair to purchase viands for their
lords, It is amusing to see the butcher-,
when they are carrying dog's flesh to any
place or when they are leading five or six
dogs to the slaughter house, for all of the
other dogs in the street, drawn by th^
cries of those going to be killed or oy
the smell of the flesh of those already
dead, fall upon the butchers, who are
obliged to go always armed with a whip
or bamboo pole to defend themselves
In China the voracity of the people ob
trudes Itself continually every Industry
or occupation seems to have such a ten
dency to the appeasing of appetite that
It becomes rather a disgusting contempla
tion. The rich are all epicures, the lower
classes sensualists. The tastes of one are
Bcarcely limited by tne extent of their
revenues, the voracity of the other un
restricted by even the nauseous species cf
food. Being the most omnivorous of all
people there Is not an animal or plant that
can be procured by art or Industry and
eaten without risk of life, which is not
pressed Into service by these gastron
omers. The flesh of wild horses Is highly
prized, as well as the larvae of the sphinx
moth and soup^made of mare's milk and
duck's blood. \r tj^V
tlon In the educated countries, because w«
subdue the coarser appetites of our na
ture. Such nations have the same natural
wants as their Eastern fellow creatures,
but the very refinement which conceals
them is also an auxiliary to the acquisi
tion of a supply of better quality.
There Is In almost every civilized coun
try a class who flaunt their poverty and
Chinese practices of the art of saving
are carried to extremes which are too
hopelessly pathetic to be comprehended
by us, the most favored and pampered
race of all humanity.
From this the rioting spread to a
revolt.
In talking with a fellow-passen
ger on tlxe China Mr. Go forth gave
the following striking story of the
awful poverty which.is gnawing at
the vitals of the masses in China,
and is the real source of their dis
content.
y NT the theory and practice of poverty
I Asia FurpaF?es herself, and the Chi
li nese coolies have made It a fine art.
Asia, the lurid continent, leads In ev
erything. All of the religions of the world
come from there— Christian, Jewish. Mo
hammedan. Buddhist, Confucian, and
nearly ell others of "the two and seventy
«ects."
The greatest hoards of both wealth and
wisdom are stored In the region east of
Buez, and around them stretch Inco-mpre
fcensibJe wastes of Ignorance and poverty,
the like whereof Is not elsewhere on the
face of the globe: so great, too, that It
xtiev In time swallow our entire civiliza
tion and leave but a blot In history which
will be known as the second dark age.
It does not follow thai the acquisition of
food is an object of less anxious atten-
A sufficient supply of wholesome food
seems to be the influencing power, the
end of all Industry In every art of the
globe, and the difference in the degrees of
avidity with which mankind pursue It is
regulated by the degree of civilization and
Intelligence which they have attained. .
Poverty's struggle for existence has
compelled these people to eat almost
every substance which is not deadly poi
son, and there is little doubt that the
narcotics with which the Asiatic dulls his
misery were discovered while some
6tarver was trying to etay his stomach
with herbs. Our wellfed American farmer
never becomes acquainted by original in
vestigation with the therapeutics of the
flora on his farm. All knowledge of the
medical properties of herbs comes from
the aborigines who were oft driven by
cruel hunger to taste every bark, root and
leaf In the search for alimentary products
of the earth.
Whether or not added poverty brought
upon the Chinese by the Indemnities
which the allies are going to collect will
deprive them of even the trifling solace
of the cricket and compel them to rely
upon the music of caged mosquitoes to
remind them of the country Is a question
that will be settled In the law foundries
of Europe, where destinies of barbarian
hordes are cast to mold.
vlda. Catching crickets and selling them
In the biff cities at five for a brass piece
has given many a Chinese country boy
his etart In life.
work, they have always had gre«.t ambi
tion to become heavy exporters, which,
means that they are, anxious to do tho
work of others as well, for a large export
trade la nothing else. Think how many
backaches It takes to produce a shipload,
of tea, for Instance, and then wonder at
the Industry which can accomplish It and
the patience which It takes to contem
plate such a herculean task without
shrinking.
The one beauty of their poverty Is th»
extreme simplicity of life to which It has
led them. Their meals are but rice, al
though boiled In a way that no European
can accomplish, and which they will not
practice unless cooking for themselves;
then a light, watery vegetable something
like a cross between our cabbage and let
tuce; with this tea. and th« same thins
for every meal twice a day the year
round, except at new year.
Clothes are as simple as the food. Two
garments of cotton are all that tho coolla
has, except hat and shoes, to put on In,
the morning: then he Is dressed. It Is this
which makes early rising so general In
the land of the rising sun. for It ts th»
horror of dressing rather than the fond
ness of sleep which keeps most of us In
bed.
Though they wear no underclothing, yet
the homespun, close-woven garments ara
warm, comfortable and healthy, as there
Is room for plenty of air circulation out
side the body and plenty of blood circu
lation within It. for lack of which we civ
ilizees suffer more from cold and colds
than from all of our bad climates put to
gether.
Perhaps In. those two attainments of
simplicity the lurid continent has, repaid,
itself for all of the sufferings undergone
in the practice of poverty as a Sno arc
together to ra&kp a board or pfflar.
Not satisfied with doing all of their own
Venders of strange viands step from
their junks to the shore, having baskets
suspended on a carrying pole, in which
are contained dogs, cats, rats or birds
either, tame or wild, generally alive; sea
slues and grubs found in the sugar cane.
The species of dogs most in request is a
Fmall spaniel. The poor animals appear
particularly dejected In their Imprison
ment, not even looking up In the hope of
freedom; while the cats, on the contrary,
keep up an Incessant squalling and seem
never to despair of escaping.
Eagles, storks, hawks and owls are
among the rarities displayed by poulter
ers, but the popular fowl In China is the
duck, which well adapts itself to the
crowded condition of the land, and takes
to the water almost from birth. The
peasants hatch the eggs in an oven or
manure heap. When the ducklings are
able to be removed, they are taken in a
boat to the nearest mudbank or heap
where, shellfish feed. There they paddle
around the fording grounds and commence
a daylong search for everything digesti
ble, paddling back at night to their con
veyances, in which they are returned

Even the Chinese in America still hank
er after the fleshpots of Chung Kwn.
and wild cats find ready sale to the Chi

The coolies of the Middle Kingdom

The poorest European beggar has him
self and usually a family, generally a
large one. Beggars are Inclined that way.
The Lord loves poor people, and so makes
many of them. «
But the beggar of Europe Is wealthy In
that he has. his freedom and family; the
Chinese coolie has sold himself, and us
ually his family as well.
With all those who are dear to him in
bondage of his own creation, himself sold
and powerless to aid them, life is a long
bitter day for him and he never fears to
part with it. So little is it his own that
ho is really less concerned than the
haughty master who rules his destiny.
If patience, humility, economy, indus
try, thrift, frugality and obedience are
virtues which will receive reward, then \*

all mortals. Those virtues have at least
made him what he Is, for none can say

Bpend the less you will have.
Ho Is 'a shining example of what comes
of following the advice of Poor Richard's
Almanac and other advisers of that whin
ing school of skim-milk philosophy. His
poverty Is, as has been shown, a minus
quantity. He has lost all he had and
half as much more, like the man In tho
algebra book who Juzzled our youth by his
woeful losses. For If the coolie could
I manage to save enough money to buy
I himself free, from his "poor little earnings
of seven cents a day,- he would still be
only a penniless beggar with a family to
support.

rived recently on the Pacific liner

pride themselves upon It. but the sons of
wart in the Middle Kingdom have no
pride, not even sorrow, in their privations
—merely the quiescent suffering of unend-
Inpr hoprl^ps misery.
Where wo boll meat and throw away the
water the Chinese eat the water with rice
and save the mjat to boll again and again
until the last shred has disappeared In
their rice pots.
While our poorest classes have the sweet
little- wild song birds to elng of the forest
in city homes, the Chinese with an equal
yearning for the music of the wilderness
can afford no such luxury, but humbly
content themselves by buying a lonely lit
tle cricket, which is put In a diminutive
cage of split bamboo fibers, where it Rings
Its short life away in a vain request for
the food which its poor bests cannot pro-

An Odd

1Ee JOURNAL JUNIOR.
Mae Harris Anson Editor
Th Journal Junior it published by Th* Minneapolis Journal fat
tb public school children of the Northwest, and is devoted prinei
pally to their own writing There is no expense attached and all
are welcome as competitors. The editor wishes to encourage corre
spondence and suggestions from teachers. All correspondence should
be addressed to the Editor Journal Junior.
WELCOME THE BOYS.
ACCOEDING
HE Junior boys have made an unusually good start
this year in writing upon the topics. Not only
have they taken to the work in greater numbers,
but their stories are better than ever before, being full
of snap and originality.
It has been commented upon several times that so
large a majority of the Junior writers are girls, and
fully two-thirds of them are prize winners. It is a fact,.
but how can it be helped when in just that percentage
the boys fail to make good? The policy of recognition is
that the most entertaining, or original, or best stories in
general shall be given the preference. Owing to lack
of space, all that merit recognition cannot be published
each week, but the work of the boys is never sacrificed
just because it is the work of boys, but because in the
points that count for good reading matter, they fail in
comparison with some girl from the same town or school.
If the boys can only keep up their present pace for
the rest of the year, the girls may find it necessary to
work harder than they have heretofore.
BEFORE THE MIBBOB.
to a man who knows how to juggle with,
figures, and who claims to have used his eyes for
seeing things, the average woman spends 100 days
of her life,if she lives to be 60standing before the
mirror. That sounds like a good deal of time spent fool
ishly, but it allows only seven minutes a day. Some
women spend too much time in "prinking," and on the
other hand there are altogether too many who do not
spend enough. There are some, for instance, whom we all
know, whose belts never "sit" properly, whose hair is
carelessly combed, whose dress-skirts sag, who have trail
ing snag ends, a button """off here, a rip carelessly
caught up there, or a dozen other small untidy details
that they would surely be ashamed of if they only took
time enough to look into the glass and see themselves as
they really are. If seven minutes a day before the
mirror are enough for a woman in which to dress her
self trigly, well and good. If not, she should take more
time, no matter what the sum total in her life of time
thus spent may amount to. Neatness and law and order
go more or less together, and a reasonable time spent in
achieving them is time well spent.
On Monday last, Budapest took a holiday
and with great enthusiasm, celebrated
"George Washington Day," heaping flow
History ers and wreaths about the newly unveiled
statue of George Washington. There were
Known. special sermons in the churches, united
choirs sang the Hungarian and American
national anthems, and tens of thousands of people lined
the streets to watch a monster parade in honor of the un
veiling upon monarchical territory of a statue erected in
honor of the first president of the United States. The
orator of the day praised Washington's work for the
cause of civilization, not only for the United States, but
for the whole world, and said that the standard he set
had influenced Lduis Kossuth, the great Hungarian pat
riot. With a monument of Washington at Caracas, Ven
ezuela, a widespread movement in Italy to celebrate
February 22 as a "peace holiday," because it is Wash
ington's birthday, and a street in a Belgium town named
for President Roosevelt, American history and what its
great lights stand for are apparently becoming pretty well
appreciated by the world at large.
A celebrated Austrian scientist claims to
have discovered a wonderful weather plant
in Cuba, which prophecies a month in ad
vance what the weather will be, and also
gives ample warning of when an earthquake
Plant. is coming and where it will strike. The
plants grow by the thousand in Cuba, he
says. What a fine thing it would be for every Junior to
have one in his window garden or in the back yard. Then
there would be no more picnics and parties spoiled by bad
weather. And from the editor's point of view, there
would be just that many less "rain" stories on all kinds
of topics, thus giving place and space to a greater va
riety of subjects. Why, the very thought of it makes
the editor feel like buying 10,000 of the plants and hav
ing a free distribution of them thruout the northwest!
The Fate of Young Billups.
Tho old-time darkies may twist long words they al
ways make their meaning plain.
"Aunt Dilsey," a young Richmond lady recently said
to her "mammy," "what has become of young Tom
Billups?"
"De Ian' sakes, Miss Baby," Aunt Dilsey replied,
with uplifted hands and eyes like saucers, "he done run
off to de great imposition out West, but we ain't heard
from him, nary line, 'cept'n 'tis one o' dese sump'n n'er
picture cards an' I jes' believe, Miss JTaby, dat he's
done been catnippedl"

THE JOURNAL JUNIOR, MINNEAPOLIS, MINNESOTA, SUNDAY MORNING, SEPTEMBER 23, 1900.
time you have a sack of peanuts, don't eat
them, but transform them into animals! What
do you think of that, boys and girls?
Suppose we get up a zoo.
What shall we have? Camels? All right. And let's
have lions, and elephants, and giraffes, and kangaroos,
and bears, and wolves, and hornets, and lobsters.
Very well. But just get the necessary materials to
gether. Somebody bring toothpicks, please, and some
body else some sheets of paper (waxed paper from the
florist's OT confectioner's would be better yet), and some
mucilage.
A few instructions will set you all going on the cor
rect path to the zoo. Suppose, for instance, we make a
hornet.
Pick out a nice smooth peanut with a crooked end
for the chest, and another slightly longer for the abdomen.
News From the Schools
LOGAN.
Constance M. Patterson, Reporter.
The fall term of 1906 opened at the Logan school with
a full complement of pupils, there being 929 in all. The
second day of the term found all rooms in good working
order. room is occupied by eighth grade pupils from
Hawthorne and Lowell schools.
Many improvements were made during the vacation
which add to the convenience and comfort of both teach
ers and scholars, ehief of which are the internal tele
phone system and new desks in many of the rooms.
Many plans are on foot among the pupils for exercises
and entertainments which will be noted in due time.
MADISON.
Mark Bohen, Reporter.
The school had a fire drill Thursday, Sept. 13. The
children did not make very good time, this being the first
drill of the year.
The cooking and manual training schools started very
nicely.
The sehool was fixed up during the summer. Most of
the seats have been varnished, and are in very good con
dition.
ADA, MINN.
Alice Rowe, Reporter.
Two weeks of work in the Ada public schools have
been completed and the high school papils were treated
to several "quizzes" Thursday and Friday.
The pupils and teacher of the English third class were
very much surprised to hear a bright Junior declare, I
didn't study that lesson I misunderstood it wrong."
Even now, pupils are worrying about being behind in
notebook work.
KENWOOD.
David Shearer, Reporter.
Sept. 4 at 10 o'clock the lines formed in front of
Kenwood school. The leader of room line as he passed
in said, "Oh~ how fine! It's all been fixed up!" The
little fellow at the end of the line emptied the contents of
his mouth (which were three marbles, a jackstone and a
top string) and muttered, "Gee!" And no wonder, for
the old schoolhouse of lasl year was shining in its fine new
paint and paper and spotless floors.
SOUTH HIGH.
Borghild Dahl, Reporter.
Pupils attending the South High school miss two fa
miliar faces, those of Miss Swain and Miss Mandeville.
Miss Burns is taking Miss Mandeville's place and Miss
Keating will impart to the ambitious of South High the
secret relations existing between lines and angles and
their cousins, squares and oblongs.
A spirit has been shown in favor of abolishing foot
ball, but nothing definite has been decided upon the sub
ject.
Nature Teaches Inventors.
"We got our hints from nature," the inventor said.
"Tak e, for instance, the hollow pillar, which is stronger

How to Make a Hornet,
Connect the two by means of a toothpick, part of which
may show, representing the hornet's narrow waist.
For the legs use a half dozen toothpicks (common
wooden ones), bending them to resemble tire real hornet's
legs as closely as possible. Fasten them into the chest
of the animal.
Use an ordinary pin for the sting. Push it thru from
the lower side of the abdomen, so that the point will
project thru the tail.
For the wings use your waxed paper, if you have it,
because it is partially transpar
ent. Cut them out according to)
the pattern given in the picture.
Paste them on the back of tho
chest. Then with pen and ink or
your paints stripe the back and
head of the hornet and thrust a
couple of pins into its head to
serve as antennae.
It is fun to tease people with,
this hornet. If they say, "Pooh,
that's just a make believe hor-
net," just prick them with the
tail end and ask them how a make
believe hornet can sting.
Then drop a little water on the
bent portion of the toothpick legs,
which will then begin to swell,
and that will cause them to
wriggle!
Now, having learned how to
make a hornet, you can go ahead
and depend on your own wits to
tell you how to make other kinds
of animals. You will probably
need other materials, such as bits
of cotton and buttons (for eyes).
These pictures will be helpful.
While some of you are busy
making the animals, suppose one
or two of you work on the cages.
Take pasteboard boxes, cut out
the covers in the form of bars,
and behold! you have perfectly good cages.
With your paints you can decorate them gayly like
circus cages, and you ean lette the name of the zoo oa,

INDIAN SE&VANT TROUBLES
The Note Was Strictly Carried Out and the Wrong Hindoo
Drank the Physic.
The author of "An Indian Garden" tells of an amus
ing experience: The old gray-bearded butler announced to me at
luncheon one day that the dish-washer was ill with fever,
but if the mistress would give some medicine he would
soon be able to resume his work. I happened to have
none by me, but as the matter was urgent, clean dishes
being important, I asked:
"Can he go to the chemisVs, do you think, for some
physic if I give him a letter! I don't know what te
write for."
"Oh, yes/' he said, "he is quite able to go that
short distance."
I thought that was much the best way, and then the
chemist could give him what was proper. So I wrote:
"Please give the bearer a dose of medicine. He says he
has fever."
I forgot to inquire about him till two days after.
Then I said:
"He is much better, your honor."
"Ah, then he took the physic?"
"No, your highness, the bazaar coolie took the physie
by mistake."
"The bazaar coolie!" I exclaimed. "What for?"
"The dish-washer said, 'Work is, therefore cannot
go myself bazaar coolie goes errands he may fetch me
the physic' So bazaar coolie took letter. Shop master
prepared physic, then told bazaar coolie to drink it.
"CooKe said, 'Not for me is the medicine, but for
another man. I take it to him.'
'Not so,' said the shop master. 'The mistress has
written, "Give to bearer," and she means you must
drink it here.'
"Many times coolie said he was not the man, but they
would not listen, and they made him drink it."
A Bird Linguist.
A bird which can talk in two languages, and whose
repertory consists of seventeen phrases, is the latest addi
tion to the Zoological Gardens, London. The bird be
longs to a species_known as the Larger Hill Mynah,
which flourishes in Northern India. Three of its phrases
are in an Indian dialect, and the rest in English. The
bird's name, as it informs all comers, is Tommy, and it
asks all visitors, "Who are you?"
The Largest Carpet
What will be the largest carpet in the world is be
ing made in London. It will cover 63,000 square feet and
will serve as the "ground" for winter sports in the
Olympxa, London. Its cost will be $25,000.

DISEASES OF* WOMEN CURED.

after working for nearly twenty
years to clear it, he learned to his
distress that, instead of becoming
smaller the obligation had steadily in
creased* "through all the years, owing
to his insignificant pay of scarcely two
pennies a day and the excessive rate
of interest.
Ram Sal saw starvation staring him
self and his family in the face, and so,
when an European, Coming to the vil
lage, told of a place across the “black
water” where a Hindoo could earn
as much as 25 cents a day and after
binding himself to work for eight
years on a plantation, would be freed
of hot he his eontraot and his debt.
Ram Sal straightway prepared to fol
low the coolie contractor whither he
led and the bunnia commanded.
The ship that Ram Sal and his
family were huddled on bore them in
course of time to Trinidad, and there,
by the terms of the contract. Ram Sal
was given quarters in a long, low
coolie barracks, from which he went
out in the mornings to toil until sun
down in the coffee and sugar fields,
the banana, orange and spice groves.
Thus he worked for six years, the
planter in the meantime feeding him
on rice and clothing him, and the
agent of the bunnia seeing to it that
until, every cent of Ram’s indebted
ness was paid not a cent of his hire

over instead to the agent by Ram's

The Vow of a Coolie.
At the end of the sixth year Ram
Sal felt the jingle of coin in his hands
for the first time, for the debt of a few
rupees that his grandfather had con
tracted when he took Ram's grand
mother to wife was wiped out. The
ring of the coins stirred up a strange
desire in the coolie’s heart—-it awak
ened his ambition, and -he vowed then
and there thiat he would be rich some
day and a planter himself.
To that goal, during the remaining
two years of his indenture. Ram Sal
saved every penny that he earned, and
his children, by their labor -in the
fields, helped increase the amount. At
the enid of that 'time it was pitiably
small—something less than a half
thousands dollars —but it was more
money than Ram Sal had ever owned
before, and, What was more important,
it was sufficient for the execution of
his'present purpose, the establishment
of himself as a silversmith in the Hin
doo quarter of Pont-of-Spain.
Ram Sal had worked at the trade in
India and was skilled in Its ways, so
before long business was plentiful
with him and he was fashioning brace
lets and anklets and necklaces and
torques of silver, and sometimes of
gold, for tihe women of the better class
of Hindoos, whose custom it is to use
their women as walking banks for
their surplus. In sihort, Ram prosper
ed exceedingly, so much so that at the
end of five years he was able to go
Into the interior and buy a run-down
plantation adjoining the one on which
he had been bound for eight ye-ws.
Hum Sul, Millionaire.
In the interim ll Ham had not forgot
ten how fields Were made to yield
their increase. Asa planter he so di
rected the labors of the! ’'hundred or
more coolies indentured to him that
his plantation soon becaifie 1 noted as
one of the most productive in the val-
Ram Sal was now fairly started on
his road to fortune, and each succeed
ing step that he took brought him
nearer it. Year by year he added io
his possessions, sometime? a planta
tion, often real estate in Port-of-Spam,
where he set himself up in a mansion,
whence he went about directing his
ever Increasing interests.
Thus Rani Sal prospered until two
years ago, when old in years, he died;
and when his heirs came to reckon up
the estate, they found it to be worth
nearly a million dollars.
It is true that Ram Sal’s efforts at
money getting were exceptional, but
it is also true that among the coolies
in Trinidad and Jamaica are many
thrifty small planters and shopkeepers
whose individual possessions are val
ued all the way from $5,000 to $50,000
on their government’s tax books. And
these Hindoos, if they had not follow
ed the contractors westward at the
bidding of their masters, the bunnlas,
would still be slaving away in the pla
teau country of their native land in
hopeless effort to clear off the inher
ited debts hanging over their heads.
That is why Mr. Chamberlain, wfio
knows these things, spoke of his king's
West Indian possessions as the coolie's
paradise. But the other man called
them the field of romance, because of
the etory of a Rajah’s soh, and many
more like unto it. Here is the story.
The Romance of (i Rajah’s Son.
The eldest son and heir of a Rajah
of one of the smaller states of Raj
putana felt so violently in love with
a beautiful maiden he had seen on
the streets of his father's capital that
he laid aside all thoughts of his own
high caste and her equally low con
dition and proceeded to woo her.
Everything went well with the lovers
for weeks, until one of the prince’s
retinue, spying on him, found him
keeping a tryst with the girl. The
Rajah was promptly informed and, en
raged at his heir’s loss of caste, de
termined to wipe out the disgrace that
the son had brought upon himself and
his family.
With the craftiness of the Oriental,
the old Rajah received his son with
all the affection of a devoted parent
until he discovered, through spies, the
next meeting place of the lovers.
Then, as they were seated In a secluded
pagoda, a band of the palace guards
burst suddenly in upon them, and
without warning first slew the maiden
and then turned upon the prince. But
the latter was too quick for the sol
diers. Seizing his sword, he managed
to cut his way clear and. knowing ev
ery Idiosyncrasy of the palace and
grounds, escaped into the city, where
he hid.
A Coolie Prince.
During the weeks thalt energetic
march was made for him toe kept him
self under cover, and not until the ef
forts of hie father relaxed d'id toe dare
crawl Par from his hading place. Even
then he was in great danger, and. real
izing that he could not hope to remain
In his father’s realm, he decided to
get as far away as possible. This he
did by disguising and presenting him
self to the nearest coolie contractor,
and a few weeks later he was on bis
way to Jamaica.
He had worked as a common field
laborer for five years before his story
accidentally became known. Over a
certain number of coolies another
coolie is usually placed as overseer.
One day the Rajah's son and his over
seer disagreed over a piece of work,
and the latter was on the point of
using a whip on the former when the
plantation's superintendent, a young
Scotsman, interfered.
Th* gratitude of the laborer was un
bounded. For once he lost Oriental
stoicism and tears came Into his eyes,
as he sold;
“Master, you have saved me from a
great disgrace.’’
The superintendent’s curiosity was
aroused, and although the Hindoo en
deavored to stink away.tv* was pressed
so hard that he finally explained his
remark.
“You have saved me from a great
disgrace,” he said. "I, a Rajah’s son,
to be struck by a Hindoo of low caste!”
Then the whole story, which the
Sootsman verified inter on, came out.
The hero of it is still employed on an
interior plantation in Jamaica.
Ram Singh's World-Wide Search
for Iboran.
Another story of the love of a man
for a woman is typical of the count
less romances that the coolies have
brought with them to their new world
homes.
Iboran was the daughter of Ohun
dar Lai, of a plateau village of India.
In the same town Ram Singh lived,
and Ram was engaged to marry Ibo
ran when he could secure rupees
enough to make the wedding festivities.
To that end he went to work for an
Englishman in a distant town. Two
years later he returned to his own vil
lage with the money only to find that
Chundar I„al and his daughter had
followed a coolie contractor tacross the
“black water.”
Ram Singh was in despair. To his
question as to where Iboran had gone
no one could definitely answer him,
not even the contractors, who had
sent the girl along hundreds of
others to the seacoast to be shipped
to any one of a half dozen places—
Fiji, Natal, Trinidad, Jamaica, British
Guiana, Montserrat.
Ram Singh vowed that great as was
the task before him he would find Ibo
ran and marry her. There was but
one wav to do that. She had become
a coolie; he must become a coolie also.
“Perhaps she has been sent to Fiji,”
the contractors told him. So Ram
Singh indentured himself for a term of
six years as a coolie on a Fiji planta
tion.
Arrived there he set about hunting
for Iboran when he was not required
to be in the fields. But his search was
in vain—she was not in Fiji.
For six years Ram Singh worked
and waited. Then “maybe she is in
Natal,” the plantation owner, who
knew his story, told him. And straight
way Ram Singh bpund himself for a
term of six years to a Natal planter.
The End of the Lover’i Travels.
Again it was six years wasted, six
years of working and waiting. But on
-the day that his contract was ended,
Ram Singh signed away his liberty tor
a third team of six years and was taken
in a coolie ship to Trinidad.
Here he got Tiis first ray of hope.
One dlay as hie was about his search, he
ran across a man who had been a
neighbor of Chundar Hal, Iboran’s fa
ther, and he tokrtijam Singh that Hor
an, mourning Tor her lover whom she
never expected to see again, had gone
with her father from India to Jamaica.
That was enough to cause the years'
necessary to his release to pass swiftly
for Ram Singh, and then, with the
money that he had saved, he took pas
sage to Jamaica and began his search
with renewed heart.
It was in an interior village that
Iboran was found, still unmarried, still
true to the memory of her lover. At
first ehe would not believe that Ram
Singh was other than a ghost, but
when he had convinced her and told
of his world-wide search for her, the
wedding festivities were straightway
planned.
To-day Ram Singfa and hie wife,
Iboran, the daughter of Cbundar Lai,
are living back of their little silver
smith shop in the town where five
years ago Ram Singh found his sweet
heart.

Tells How to Die Poor
By LAWRENCE PORCHER HEXT.
“Th’ paapers says,” remarked O’Flynn,
“that lots av millionaires
Is worrit wit’ th’ wealth they’ve got—
it loads thim down wit’ careß.”
"Perhaps it does,” responded Mack,
“an' some av thim I see.
Ixpriss th’ wish to die in peace an’
utter poverty. *
“Some millionaires—we'll call no names
—has so much surplus wealth
That it has preyed upon their minds,
and much impaired their health;
They have- th' noble wish to die lik*
paupers, so ’tis said,
But, do ye moind, they scramble f’r
th’ gold until they're dead.
“They spint th’ spring-time av their
lives in hot pursuit av wealth;
An’ while they strove to win th’ race,
old age crept on be stealth;
They passed the milestones wan by
wan; they broke th’ ties that
bind;
They won th’ race, an’ wealth is theirs,
but youth is left behind.
"Th’ sugar is not half so sweet as
’twas whin they was young,
An’ jlst th' faintest scint of wine will
serve to tie th’ tongue;
They have th’ wealth they coveted
whin they was naught but boys,
But, wit’ it all, they’ve found no peace,
no happiness, no Joys.
“An’ now they say they’ll spind their
wealth before their days are
done.
So whin they die they will have reach
ed th’ place where they begun;
But though they leave no wealth be
hind, their gold has made a name,
FT they have etched their autographs
on ivry dome av fame.
"Ye ask me if they are sincere? I
think they must be so,
F’r afther death they'll need no wealth,
no matter where they go;
They have enough to serve their wants,
so long as they’re on earth,
An’ whin they die, no wan wiil ask
how much th’ corpse is worth.
"Th’ paapers may have told th’ truth
about the millionaires;
Too much av gold perhaps begets a
multitude av cares;
But ’tis not thrue that manny men
are spinding phwat they’ve got—
Th’ most av thim are strugglin’ to in
crease their worldy lot.
An’ ’tis but right that ivry man should
want his share av gold
F’r In this counthry av th’ free all
things are bought an’ sold;
An’ though ’tis aizy f'r us all to die
wit'out a cint,
’Tis hard f’r anny wan to live wit’
poverty contint.
“I have no fault to find wit’ thim that
have too much, av wealth;
I much prafer me peaceful sleep, me
hopes, an’ me good health;
But, if they want to leave good deeds
instead av wealth behind.
Wit’ means, they’s always' lots av
ways, an’ none too hard to find.
“But ain’t they some," inquired Pat,
“that can’t, before they die,
Spind’aU th’ wealth they’ve gathered
up, no matter how they try?”
"Av course they is,” responded Mack,
“on that ye may daypind,
But honest min need have no fear av
facing such an ind/’

A Tranedy of Treat Stream.
W. D. Hulbert, in Leslie’s Monthly.
A big. brown and white bird came
sailing up the trout stream, his long
wings stretched far out to right and
left. He flew slowly, for he was look
ing for something—something that he
wanted very much. He was doing the
family marketing. Up in the top of a
big pine tree, a mile away, his wife
was sitting on a nest full of eggs, and
it was both his duty and his pleasure
to find a supper for her. Suddenly he
stopped short. He had caught sight of
the thing for which he was searching
—a dusky, shadowy shape with an out
line like that of a submarine torpedo
boat, lying moveless in the clear wa
ter. For Just an instant he seemed to
hang poised in the air, but It was only
long enough to change the direction of
his motion; then down he went with a
rush and a swoop.
The brook trout saw him coming
and tried to dart away, but It was too
late.
With a mighty splash the osprey
struck the stream and went clear un
der and out of sight, while the water
boiled and surged over him. He could
not see for the commotion about him,
but his aim had been true, and his
outstretched feet touched a slippery,
slimy, wriggling body that was just
beginning to gather headway.
Quicker than a wink his toes closed
about it and his sharp talons sank
deep into the trout’s flesh. Then up he
came, rising out of the stream like
some fabled monster of old, and shak
ing the water from his feathers in a
shower of flying drops. Every thread
of muscle In his wings and breast was
working with all its might to lift that
heavy trout. Up went the bird's
great pinions till they were straight
above him; then down the came, lash
ing the air like whips. Up again and
down, up and down, up and down,
harder and faster and fiercer; and lit
tle by little he and his victim rose
above the stream, till at last they
were clear of the treetopa. Then
straight away to the nest in the old
pine, where th* wife was waiting to
wake them both welcome.

Where Flesh and Blood Are Cheap
Chinese Coolies, Unskilled, Underpaid, Underfed —Ricksha Coolies of the Seaports
harming, Sedan and Wheelbarrow Coolies—Their Wages, Food and Clothing.
SEDAN AND ’RIKISHA COOLIES ON HANKOW'S BUND. HERE ONLY CHINESE USE SEDAN CHAIRS. NOTE PALM AND PINE OROWINO
SIDE BY SIDE.
BY ELIZABETH NOYES
HEMPSTONE.
I.
Hankow, China, April 24, 1925.
THERE are two descriptive
words that always spring to
mind when thinking of China
or of ants; they are “teem
ing” and “swarming.” China is teem
ing and swarming with scrawny,
yellow people, like—oh, very like —
ants. They are the coolies—the
unskilled laborers; and they are
found everywhere. In the ports they
plod wearily back and forth, to and
from ships. bearing unbelievable
loads; doing the work of horses and
machines. In the cities they dash
about, drawing rickshas or harnessed
to wheelbarrows. On the farms their
only riva-ls are the big water buffa
loes—patient, uncomplaining. Sailing
up and down the rivers, on the high
seas, living and dying in sampans
and junks, are the coolies. In the
Summer, glittering with sweat, their
slender naked backs are burnished
